title: SQL Stored Procedures & Functions
description: Creating and using stored procedures, user-defined functions, error handling, and performance.

questions.text: What is a stored procedure in SQL Server?

options.text: A saved SELECT query only
A precompiled set of one or more SQL statements stored in the database that can be executed by name
A function that always returns a table
A trigger that fires on data changes

questions.text: Which syntax correctly creates a stored procedure named GetActiveEmployees?

options.text: CREATE PROC GetActiveEmployees AS BEGIN SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Active = 1 END
MAKE PROCEDURE GetActiveEmployees AS SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE Active = 1
STORE PROCEDURE GetActiveEmployees AS SELECT * FROM Employees
CREATE STORED PROC GetActiveEmployees BEGIN SELECT * END

questions.text: How do you execute a stored procedure called GetAllProducts in SQL Server?

options.text: RUN GetAllProducts
CALL GetAllProducts
EXEC GetAllProducts
START GetAllProducts

questions.text: How do you pass an input parameter to a stored procedure? Example: procedure GetEmployeeByDept(@DeptID INT)

options.text: EXEC GetEmployeeByDept DeptID = 5
EXEC GetEmployeeByDept @DeptID = 5
EXEC GetEmployeeByDept (5)
Both b and c are valid

questions.text: How is an OUTPUT parameter defined in a stored procedure?

options.text: @ParamName DataType RETURN
@ParamName DataType OUTPUT
@ParamName DataType OUT
RETURN @ParamName DataType

questions.text: What is the main difference between a stored procedure and a scalar user-defined function (UDF)?

options.text: Stored procedures cannot have parameters; UDFs can
A scalar UDF returns a single value and can be used in a SELECT expression; a stored procedure cannot be used inside a SELECT expression
Stored procedures can return tables; UDFs cannot
UDFs can use transactions; stored procedures cannot

questions.text: What does a scalar user-defined function return?

options.text: A table result set
A single scalar value of a defined data type
Multiple values as OUTPUT parameters
A status code only

questions.text: What is an inline table-valued function (TVF)?

options.text: A function that returns a single row
A function that contains a single SELECT statement and returns a table result — equivalent to a parameterized view
A function with multiple SELECT statements and a RETURN TABLE statement
A function stored inside a table

questions.text: What is a multi-statement table-valued function?

options.text: A TVF with only one SELECT statement
A TVF that uses multiple statements to build and return a table variable
A function that calls multiple stored procedures
A function returning multiple scalar values

questions.text: What does the RETURN statement do in a stored procedure?

options.text: Returns a result set to the caller
Exits the procedure immediately and optionally returns an integer status code
Returns a table variable
Commits any open transaction

questions.text: What is the purpose of TRY...CATCH in SQL Server?

options.text: To improve query performance
To handle runtime errors — code in TRY runs normally; if an error occurs, execution jumps to CATCH
To catch duplicate key violations only
To create transaction savepoints automatically

questions.text: What does THROW do in SQL Server error handling?

options.text: Logs the error to the Windows Event Log
Re-raises the current error or raises a new error with specified message, severity, and state
Terminates the session
Converts an error to a warning

questions.text: What is the difference between THROW and RAISERROR in SQL Server?

options.text: RAISERROR is the newer syntax; THROW is deprecated
THROW is simpler, re-raises the original error with its state, and abides by SET XACT_ABORT; RAISERROR is older with more formatting options
THROW only works in stored procedures; RAISERROR works everywhere
They are identical

questions.text: What does @@ROWCOUNT contain after a DML statement?

options.text: The total number of rows in the table
The number of rows affected by the most recent DML statement
The number of rows returned by the last SELECT
The row count of the last table scanned

questions.text: What does @@ERROR contain after a statement executes?

options.text: The error message text of the last error
The error number of the last error (0 if no error)
The line number where the last error occurred
The severity of the last error

questions.text: What is a recompile hint for a stored procedure used for?

options.text: To force the procedure to recompile and generate a new execution plan each time it runs, avoiding bad cached plans
To compile the procedure for the first time
To update the stored procedure's code
To enable parallel execution

questions.text: Which syntax adds a recompile hint to a stored procedure execution?

options.text: EXEC MyProc WITH RECOMPILE
EXEC MyProc OPTION (RECOMPILE)
Both a and b are valid in different contexts
EXEC RECOMPILE MyProc

questions.text: What is a key performance advantage of stored procedures over ad-hoc SQL?

options.text: Stored procedures always run faster regardless of query complexity
Execution plans for stored procedures are cached and reused, reducing parse/compile overhead on subsequent calls
Stored procedures bypass the query optimizer
Stored procedures use less memory

questions.text: Can a stored procedure modify data (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)?

options.text: No, stored procedures are read-only
Yes, stored procedures can execute any valid SQL including DML and DDL statements
Only if the procedure is defined with MODIFIABLE keyword
Only SELECT is allowed inside stored procedures

questions.text: What functions are NOT allowed inside a user-defined scalar function in SQL Server?

options.text: String manipulation functions like UPPER() and LEN()
Non-deterministic functions like GETDATE() and RAND() are restricted; DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) is not allowed
Mathematical functions like ROUND() and ABS()
Aggregate functions like SUM() and AVG()

questions.text: What does ALTER PROCEDURE do?

options.text: Drops and recreates the procedure, losing permissions
Modifies the definition of an existing stored procedure while preserving existing permissions
Renames the stored procedure
Copies the stored procedure to another database

questions.text: Which function inside a CATCH block returns the error message text?

options.text: @@ERROR
ERROR_NUMBER()
ERROR_MESSAGE()
@@ERRORMESSAGE

questions.text: What is parameter sniffing in the context of stored procedures?

options.text: A security vulnerability where parameters are exposed
SQL Server compiles the execution plan using the parameter values supplied at first execution; this plan may be suboptimal for different parameter values later
A method for automatically detecting missing parameters
A technique for logging procedure parameters

questions.text: Which inline TVF syntax is more efficient than a multi-statement TVF and why?

options.text: Multi-statement TVF because it allows complex logic
Inline TVF because the optimizer can expand it inline and apply predicate pushdown, unlike multi-statement TVF which is treated as a black box
They have identical performance
Multi-statement TVF because it caches results

questions.text: What does sp_helptext do in SQL Server?

options.text: Returns the text/definition of a stored procedure or view
Lists all stored procedures in the database
Returns help documentation for SQL syntax
Returns the execution plan of a stored procedure

questions.text: Can a user-defined function call a stored procedure?

options.text: Yes, always
No — SQL Server user-defined functions cannot execute stored procedures
Only if the stored procedure is read-only
Only scalar functions can call stored procedures

questions.text: What is the WITH ENCRYPTION option when creating a stored procedure?

options.text: Encrypts the data returned by the procedure
Encrypts the procedure's source code definition so it cannot be viewed with sp_helptext
Requires encrypted connections to execute the procedure
Encrypts the parameters passed to the procedure

questions.text: What is the difference between EXEC and sp_executesql for dynamic SQL?

options.text: EXEC supports parameterized queries; sp_executesql does not
sp_executesql supports parameterized queries allowing plan reuse and preventing SQL injection; EXEC concatenates strings directly
They are identical
sp_executesql is deprecated

questions.text: What does the SCHEMABINDING option do when creating a function or view?

options.text: Binds the function to a specific database schema for security
Prevents underlying tables from being altered or dropped in ways that would break the function/view definition
Binds the function to a specific user
Makes the function visible only within its schema

questions.text: Which system view shows all stored procedures in the current SQL Server database?

options.text: sys.tables
sys.procedures
sys.objects WHERE type = 'P'
Both b and c return stored procedures
